Facts.text: Roman consuls begin their year in office
Origin of the Christian Era
Roman garrison of Mainz uprising
Governor Lucius Antonius Saturninus of Germany becomes emperor of Rome
Start of Roman (Pontifical) Indiction
Co-emperors and brothers Valentinian I and Valens are made consul to mark their new reign and shortly after dividing the empire into East and West
Last gladiator competition in Rome
Gallo-Roman scholar Sidonius Apollinaris recites a panegyric in verse in honor of the Emperor Avitus in Rome
Gallo-Roman scholar Sidonius Apollinaris recites a panegyric in verse in honor of the Emperor Anthemius, his third panegyric, in Rome
Conquest of Mecca: Prophet Muhammad sets out with an army 10,000 strong to conquer Mecca
Hofmeier Charles Martel flees from Bishop Willibrord
Russia adopts Julian calendar
Michael VIII Palaiologos is proclaimed co-emperor of the Empire of Nicaea with his ward John IV Laskaris
Jews of Sicily are no longer required to attend conversionist services
Albert II of Habsburg crowned King of Hungary after the death of his father-in-law King Sigismund
Juw Dekama elected potentate of Frisia
Jews are expelled from Laibach in Austria
Croatian nobles elect Ferdinand I of Austria as king of Croatia in the Parliament on Cetin
Geuzen sets fire to Woudrichem
Korean naval commander Admiral Yi Sun-sin begins his famous war diary "Nanjung ilgi" (included in UNESCO's Memory of the World 2013)
Scotland begins its numbered year on January 1 instead of March 25
Papal Chancery adopts January 1 as the beginning of the year (previously March 25)
Academia de Bellas Artes is founded in Seville with painter Bartolomé Esteban Murillo its first president

English Colonel George Monck, with two brigades of troops, crosses the Anglo-Scottish border by fording the River Tweed at Coldstream and heads through knee-deep snow to London to end military rule and restore the monarchy
Regular mail delivery begins between New York and Boston
Don Carlos de Gurrea becomes Spanish land guardian of Southern Netherlands
Protestant Western Europe (except England) begins using the Gregorian calendar
Russia begins using the Anno Domini era and no longer uses the Anno Mundi era of the Byzantine Empire
John V succeeds his father Pedro II as King of Portugal
Parisian merchant Jean Marius obtains a 5-year royal privilege for his invention of a folding umbrella, the first in Europe
French explorer Jean-Baptiste Charles Bouvet de Lozier discovers the uninhabited Bouvet Island, the world's most remote island, near Antarctica
French explorers Louis-Joseph and François de La Vérendrye are the first Europeans to reach the foot of the Rocky Mountains at the Yellow River, having crossed the Great Plains
Date of action in the opera "Madeleine"
First traveler's cheques go on sale in London, can be used in 90 European cities
John Walter publishes the 1st issue of his newspaper "The Daily Universal Register", renamed "The Times" of London in 1788
London newspaper "The Daily Universal Registrar" changes name to "The Times"
Quakers in Pennsylvania emancipate their slaves
Albany replaces New York City as capital of New York
Russia appoints 1st Jewish censor to censor Hebrew books
Dutch East Indies Company dissolves
The dwarf planet Ceres is discovered by Italian monk Giuseppe Piazzi
Emperor Gia Long orders all bronze wares of the Tây Sơn Dynasty to be collected and melted into nine cannons for the Royal Citadel in Huế, Vietnam
Haiti gains independence from France (National Day), making it the only state ever founded by former slaves and without slavery
World's first iron bridge opens to the public, crossing the Severn River at Coalbrookdale, England. Regarded as the symbol of the Industrial Revolution.
Curacao is taken by English (until March, 1816)
African Benevolent Society (education) forms
Congress prohibits importation of slaves
Sierra Leone becomes a British colony
Holland Brigade under Brigadier-General David Hendrik Chassé reaches Madrid (Napoleonic Wars)
Field marshal Blucher's troops cross the Rhine at Kaub
Official reopening of the White House
Godert van der Capellen resigns as governor of Dutch-Indies
Dutch Trade Company NHM gets opium monopoly on Java
Slave plantation owner Charles Farquharson begins his diary at Prospect Hill Plantation, Watlings Island (San Salvador). Only plantation diary to survive from the Bahamas (ends Dec 1832).
British government demands Falkland islands
Curacao census: 2,602 whites, 6,531 free people, 5,894 slaves
German Tolunie goes into effect
1st official horse race in South Australia-Adelaide
1st recorded bowling match in US, Knickerbocker Alleys, NYC
1st illustrated weekly magazine in US publishes 1st issue, NYC
1st edition of New Rotterdam's Daily (3x per week)
The Cobble Hill Tunnel in Brooklyn is completed
Yucatan declares independence from Mexico
Michigan is 1st state to abolish capital punishment
Britain takes Mosquito Coast from Nicaragua
City of Glasgow steamer inaugurates Philadelphia-Liverpool line
1st US public bath opens in New York City
National debt of Britain & Ireland is 765,126,582 pounds
Netherlands begins issuing postage stamps
1st practical fire engine (horse-drawn) in US enters service
Presbyterian minister John Miller Dickey and his wife, Sarah Emlen Cresson, found Ashmun Institute, a Black college (later Lincoln University),in Hinsonville, Pennsylvania
Canada begins using decimal currency system
Slavery abolished in the Dutch East Indies for areas under direct rule
Battle of Galveston, Texas-Confederates recapture the city
First homestead under the Homestead Act is claimed near Beatrice, Nebraska
Belgium disbands salt tax
Moerdijk railway bridge over Hollands Diep, South Holland opens - then longest bridge in Europe
Origin of Japanese Era
New York City annexes the Bronx
Britain's Midlands Railway abolishes second class travel, ending the practice of carrying third class passengers in open air wagons
The Reichsbank, central bank of the German Empire, opens in Berlin
Building of Panama Canal begins
Eritrea is consolidated into a colony by the Italian government
The Rose Parade, then known as the Tournament of Roses, is first held in Pasadena, California
French troops occupy Nioro, West-Sudan, 3000 killed
Ellis Island opens as a US immigration inspection station, becomes the gateway to the US for more than 12 million people
1st US college extension courses for credit, University of Chicago
Japan adopts the Gregorian calendar
The railway line from Germiston to Pretoria, South Africa, is opened to traffic
Denmark adopts Mid-European time
Manchester Ship Canal in England opens to traffic
South African Amateur Athletic Union is founded in Johannesburg
Norway adopts Mid-European time
1st football game between black colleges-Atlanta U 10, Tuskegee 0
Brooklyn merges with NY to form present City of NY
Lightship replaces whistling buoy at mouth of San Francisco Bay
Cuba liberated from Spanish rule by the US, American occupation continues till 1902
British protectorates of Northern and Southern Nigeria are established
Compulsory education in the Netherlands goes into effect
The Commonwealth of Australia is formed when the British (Imperial) Parliament Act, the Commonwealth of Australia Constitution Act 1900, comes into effect
1st Rose Bowl, Tournament Park, Pasadena, California: Michigan beats Stanford, 49-0; MVP: Neil Snow, Michigan, FB
Nathan Stubblefield makes 1st public demonstration of radio, Pennsylvania
Dutch East Indies government takes control of opium distribution
Dutch law makes a driver's license mandatory
First permanent movie theater in Canada, The Ouimetoscope, is opened by Léo Ouimet in Montreal, Quebec
Poll tax of £1 per head on all adult male inhabitants of Natal, South Africa becomes payable, except for indentured Indians and married Blacks, imposed by the Natal parliament in 1905
Joe Gans lands a devastating right to the head of Canadian challenger Kid Herman to retain his world lightweight boxing title with an 8th round knockout in Tonopah, Nevada
US PresidentTheodore Rooseveltshakes a record 8,513 hands in 1 day
American Robert Fowler runs then world record marathon (2:52:45.4) at Yonkers, NY
Drilling begins on the Lakeview Gusher
In Great Britain, the Old Age Pension Law is finally instituted, providing pensions for every British subject over 70 with low income
England cricket underhand bowler George Simpson-Hayward takes 6-43 on debut with underarm lobs in 1st Test against South Africa in Johannesburg; SA wins by 19 runs
Belgian mining law introduces a 9½-hour workday
South Australia transfers Northern Territory to federal government
First running of San Francisco's famed "Bay to Breakers" footrace (7.63 miles/12.3 km); Robert Vlught wins in 44:10
US Post office begins parcel post deliveries
Klaas ter Laan becomes The Netherlands first socialist mayor (Zaandam)
Northern and Southern Nigeria are united in British colony of Nigeria
St. Petersburg–Tampa Airboat Line becomes the world's first scheduled airline, covering the 18-mile distance in 23 minutes, piloted by Tony Jannus. Service ceases after three months
Jews of Laibach Austria expelled
First blood transfusion using stored and cooled blood is performed
First issue of "Journal of Negro History" is published
Last day of the Julian calendar in Finland
Battle of Jutland British naval commanderDavid Beattyis promoted to full admiral
Belorussian SSR established
Belarusian Communist Organization is founded as a separate party
Coal miners in the Transvaal, South Africa, embark on a strike in response to a wage cut, which quickly escalated into a large-scale revolt against the government, known as the Rand Rebellion.
Vancouver, British Columbia starts driving on the right side of road
Britain's Railways are grouped into the Big Four: LNER, GWR, SR, and LMSR
Union of Socialist Soviet Republics established
Grossdeutsche Volksgemeinschaft/Volkische Block replaces NSDAP
Norway's capital Christiania changes name to Oslo
Flood in Rhine strikes Cologne
Communist uprising in West Java
MLB Brooklyn Robins announce release of future Baseball Hall of Fame outfielder Zack Wheat after 18 consecutive seasons with the team; Wheat hits .324 in final season with Philadelphia A's
Turkey adopts the Gregorian calendar: December 18, 1926 (Julian), is immediately followed by January 1, 1927 (Gregorian)
1st US air-conditioned office building opens, San Antonio
Algemeene Vereeniging Radio Omroep (AVRO) begins broadcasting in the Netherlands
Claus von Stauffenberg promoted to 2nd lieutenant
Jurgens & Van den Berg merge with Lever Brothers to form Unilever
Jacob Cocey Sr chosen as Mayor of Massillon, Ohio
United States Post Office Department issues a set of 12 stamps commemorating the 200th anniversary of George Washington's birth
Alcatraz officially becomes a federal prison
Federal Deposit Insurance Corp (US bank guarantor) effective
International Telecommunication Union established
Nazi Germany passes the "Law for the Prevention of Genetically Diseased Offspring"
1st Orange Bowl: Bucknell beats Miami (FL), 26-0
Associated Press inaugurates Wirephoto
Eastern Airlines hires Eddie Rickenbacker as GM
President Mustapha Kemal Pasha names himself "Ataturk: Father of Turkey"
1st newspaper to microfilm its current issues, NY Herald Tribune
Anastasio Somoza García becomes President of Nicaragua
German officer Claus von Stauffenberg promoted to captain
Safety glass in vehicle windscreens becomes mandatory in the UK
US Army Air Corps physiological research laboratory is completed in Ohio
Sydney, Australia, swelters in 45°C (113°F) heat, a record for the city
The Netherlands begins taxing wages
Triumphal procession for the Roman GeneralGaius Mariuswith the defeated Numidian King Jugurtha led in chains though Rome
Julian calendar takes effect for the first time by edict of Roman dictatorJulius Caesar
Commodus, son of EmperorMarcus Aureliusbecomes consul for the first time - at 15 then youngest ever in Roman history
Portuguese expedition underPedro Álvares Cabralare the first Europeans to discover the Bay of Guanabara naming it Rio de Janeiro after mistaking it for a river entrance
French KingLouis XIIloses Gaeta, his last remaining territory in the Kingdom of Naples
Francis, Duke of Angouleme succeeds KingLouis XIIasFrancis I of France
Charles IIStuart crowned King of Scotland at Scone Abbey
First entry in the diary of English civil servantSamuel Pepys
Rebels within the New Model Army, led by GeneralThomas Fairfax, occupy York
Jean Racine's five-act tragedy play "Bajazet" premieres in Paris, France
GlassblowerDaniel Gabriel Fahrenheitproposes system for making thermometers and the Fahrenheit temperature scale in a paper to the Royal Society of London and is elected a fellow on its basis

International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature establishes the "starting point" for standardized species names across the animal kingdom based on the binomial nomenclature in the 10th edition of Systema Naturae by CarolusLinnaeus
English potterJosiah Wedgwoodwrites that he has developed his famous 'wedgwood blue' color in a letter to Thomas Bentley

1,500 soldiers of the 6th Pennsylvania Regiment under GeneralAnthony Wayne's command rebel against the Continental Army's winter camp in Morristown, New Jersey as part of the Pennsylvania Mutiny of 1781
Napoléon Bonaparte abolishes the French Republican calendar after 12 years of use
Mary Shelley's "Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus" is published anonymously by the small London publishing house of Lackington, Hughes, Harding, Mavor, & Jones
William Lloyd Garrisonpublishes 1st issue of abolitionist newspaper "The Liberator" in Boston; publication continued until the 13th Amendment abolished slavery in 1865
Porfirio Díazconquers Mexico City
General Sherman's Union army begins its Carolinas campaign (lasts until Apr 26th)
War of the Triple Alliance: Paraguay's capital Asunción falls to Brazilian forces led by General João de Souza da Fonseca Costa
Queen Victoriaproclaimed Empress of India
Johannes Brahms' Violin Concerto in D major premieres in Leipzig
Ambrose Bierceis appointed editor of "The Wasp" magazine
Dr John Watson is first introduced to Sherlock Holmes in story written byArthur Conan Doyle
First public performance of Czech composerAntonín Dvořák's String Quartet No. 12 "The American", by the Kneisel Quartet, in Boston, Massachusetts
Philippine RevolutionaryEmilio Aguinaldobecomes a Freemason, joining Pilar Lodge No. 203, Imus, Cavite
German physicistWilhelm Röntgenannounces his discovery of x-rays
Italian poet and playwrightGabriele D'Annunzio's dramatic work "Sogno d'un mattino di primavera," (Dream of a Spring Morning) premieres in Rome
First date inJohn Dos Passos' USA trilogy (The 42nd Parallel) [Fictional]
In Delhi, a great durbar, or formal reception, marks the coronation of KingEdward VIIas Emperor of India; the British release some 16,000 prisoners in honor of the occasion
PresidentTheodore RooseveltandEdith Rooseveltunveil new renovations to The White House, including a new West Wing

Jack Hobbsmakes his international debut in England's 2nd Test win over Australia at the MCG, scoring 83 and 28 in his two innings. Goes onto become the leading run scorer and century maker in 1st-class cricket history.
British naval officerDavid Beattyis promoted to rear-admiral
Sun Yat-senforms the Republic of China
D.W. Griffithshows "The Clansman" at a sneak preview
T. E. Lawrencejoins the forces of the Arabian sheik Feisal al Husayn, beginning his adventures that will lead him to Damascus by October, 1918
Edsel Ford succeeds his father,Henry Ford, as president of the Ford Motor Company
Rasse und Siedlungshauptamt publishesHimmler's wedding laws - SS men have to prove their Aryan descent in order to have children
Hewlett-Packardis founded byBill Hewlettand Dave Packard in a garage in Palo Alto, California "the birthplace of Silicon Valley"
Russian generalGeorgy Zhukovappointed chief of general staff
1st day of the Gregorian calendar in Holland & Flanders
The Irish Parliament votes to join the Kingdom of Great Britain, forming the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland
Emancipation Proclamation is issued byAbraham Lincolnto free slaves in Confederate states
Ali al-Rida [Abū al-Ḥasan al-Thānī], 8th Imam of Twelver Shia Islam and descendant of the prophet Muhammad, born in Medina, Abbasid Caliphate (d. 818) [approximate birthdate]
Alexander VI [Rodrigo Borgia], Spanish born Pope controversial for acknowledging his children (1492-1503), born in Xàtiva, Spain (d. 1503)
Sigismund I the Old, King of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania (1506-48), born in Kozienice, Poland (d. 1548)
Huldrych Zwingli, Swiss Christian theologian, musician, and leader of the Swiss Protestant Reformation, born in Wildhaus, Switzerland (d. 1531)
Caspar Cruciger, German church reformer, born in Leipzig (d. 1548)
Henry, Duke of Cornwall, First living child of King Henry VIII and Catherine of Aragon, born in Richmond Palace, Surrey, England (d. 1511)
Margaret Leijonhufvud, Queen consort of Gustav I of Sweden, born in Ekeberg Castle, Sweden (d. 1551)
Francois Baudouin [Franciscus Balduinus], French lawyer and humanist (controversial Calvinist who converted to Catholicism), born in Arras, France (d. 1573)
Stephen Bocskai, Prince of Transylvania and Hungary (1605-6), born in Kolozsvár, Eastern Hungarian Kingdom (d. 1606)
Philippus Rovenius, Dutch vicar who led the Catholic church in the Netherlands, born in Deventer (d. 1651)
Friedrich Spanheim, German theologian, Calvinistic professor, and writer (Dubia Evangelica, Disputatio de Gratia Universali), born in Amberg, Bavaria (d. 1649)[1]
Bartolomé Esteban Murillo, Spanish Baroque artist (The Vision of St. Anthony), born in Seville, Spain (d. 1682) [baptizism date]
Isaac Sweers, Dutch fleet admiral, born in Nijmegen, Dutch Republic (d. 1673)
Christoph Bernhard, German baroque composer, born in Kolberg, Pomerania (d. 1692)
Antoinette du Ligier de la Guard Deshoulieres, French poet and literary figure, born in Paris (d. 1694)
Go-Sai [Nagahito], 111th Emperor of Japan (1655-63), born in Kyōto, Japan (d. 1685)

Christian Thomasius, German jurist, philosopher, and co-founder of the University of Halle (Essays on Church, State, and Politics), born in Leipzig, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1728)[1][2]
Arnold Drakenborch, Dutch classical scholar who studied philology and law (Livy, Silius Italicus), born in Utrecht, Dutch Republic (d. 1748)[1]
Johann Pfeiffer, German violinist, composer and concert master at court (Bayreuth), born in the Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1761)
Soame Jenyns, British writer (View of the Internal Evidence of the Christian Religion) and Member of Parliament (1741-80), born in London, England (d. 1787)[1]
Franz von der Trenck, Austrian soldier whose unit is considered one of the worst in European history due to insubordination and plundering, born in Reggio di Calabria (d. 1749)
Kristijonas Donelaitis, Lithuanian poet (The Seas) and Lutheran pastor, born in Lasdinehlen, East Prussia (d. 1780)
Christian Friedrich Gregor, Moravian composer and bishop, born in Dirsdorf, Silesia (d. 1801)

Edmund Burke, British statesman, philosopher and author (A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of Our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful, Reflections on the Revolution in France) [NS=Jan 12], born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1797)
Archibald Bulloch, American lawyer, Revolutionary War military officer, and politician (Governor of Georgia, 1776-77), born in Charlestown, Province of South Carolina (d. 1777)
Giovanni Furno, Italian composer and music teacher in Naples, born in Capua, Kingdom of Sicily (d. 1837)
Frederick Muhlenberg, American politician and first speaker of the US House of Representatives (1789-91, 1793-95), born in Trappe, Pennsylvania (d. 1801)[1]
Johannes Kinker, Dutch linguist, philosopher and poet (Minderjarige Zangster), born in Nieuwer-Amstel (d. 1845)
Maria Edgeworth, Anglo-Irish novelist and educator (Castle Rackrent, Ennui), born in Black Bourton, England (d. 1849)
Georges Cadoudal, Breton politician and royalist who plotted to overthrow Napoleon, born in Brech, Brittany, Kingdom of France (d. 1804)
André Marie Constant Duméril, French zoologist (Zoologie analytique), born in Amiens, France (d. 1860)
Micah Hawkins, American poet, playwright and composer, born in Head of the Harbour, New York (d. 1825)
William Clowes, British printer and founder of the printing firm William Clowes Ltd. in 1803, born in Chichester, England (d. 1847)[1]
William Beale, English composer and baritone, born in Lankrake, Cornwall, England (d. 1854)
Henrik Anker Bjerregaard, Norwegian writer and poet (Fjeldeventyret), born in Ringsaker, Norway (d. 1842)
Sydney Nelson, English song composer (Rose of Allendale), born in London, England (d. 1862)
Václav Emanuel Horák, Czech composer, chiefly of liturgical music, organist, and kapellmeister, born in Lobeč, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1871)
John Pieter Heije, Dutch physician, writer and poet (Silver Fleet), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1876)[1]
Hong Xiuquan, Chinese rebel and religious leader (Taiping Rebellion), born in Hua County, Guangdong, Qing Dynasty (d. 1864)
Charles Renouvier, French philosopher, born in Montpellier, France (d. 1903)
Arthur Hugh Clough, English poet, friend of Matthew Arnold, born in Liverpool, England (d. 1861)
George Foster Shepley, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Saco, Maine (d. 1878)
Tom Hyer, American bare-knuckle boxer (American heavyweight champion 1841-51), born in Caernarvon Township, Pennsylvania (d. 1864)
Sándor Petőfi, Hungarian poet (A virágnak megtiltani nem lehet - You Cannot Forbid the Flower), and revolutionary, born in Kiskőrös, Kingdom of Hungary, Austrian Empire (d. 1849)
William Lewis Cabell ", Old Tige" American Brigadier General (Confederate Army) and Mayor of Dallas, born in Danville, Virginia (d. 1911)
William Joseph Westbrook, British organist and sacred music composer, born in London, England (d. 1894)
Robert Lawson, Scottish-New Zealand architect (Dunedin's First Church, Larnach Castle), born in Newburgh, Fife, Scotland (d. 1902)
Ludovic Halévy, French author and playwright (contributions to Carmen and La belle Hélène), born in Paris, France (d. 1908)[1]
William Hugh Young, American Brigadier General in the Confederate Army who fought in the Battle of Shiloh, born in Booneville, Missouri (d. 1901)
Nikolay Lodizhensky, Russian diplomat and composer, born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1916)
Nikola Pasic, Serbian nationalist and premier (over many periods from 1891 to 1926), born in Zaječar, Serbia (d. 1926)
John W. Goff, American lawyer and judge known for his part in the Catalpa Rescue, born in Gorey, Ireland (d. 1924)[1][2]
Benjamin Holt, American businessman (Holt Manufacturing; Caterpillar), and inventor (crawler-type tread tractor), born in Concord, New Hampshire (d. 1920)
Eugène-Anatole Demarçay, French chemist and discoverer of the element europium, born in Paris, France (d. 1903)
Hans Koessler, German composer, conductor and music teacher, born in Waldeck, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1926)
James George Frazer, Scottish anthropologist and folklorist (The Golden Bough), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1941)
Charles Seeberger, American inventor (coined term escalator and designed 1st commercially produced escalator), born in Oskaloosa, Iowa (d. 1931)[1]
Michael J. Owens, American glassmaker and inventor (glass bottle-making machine), born in Mason County, West Virginia (d. 1923)[1]
Jesse Reno, American engineer and inventor (created the first escalators), born in Ft. Leavenworth, Kansas (d. 1947)[1]
Aleko Konstantinov, Bulgarian writer (To Chicago & Back), born in Svishtov, Ottoman Empire (d. 1897)
Pierre de Coubertin, French educator and historian (founder International Olympic Committee; President IOC 1896–1925), born in Paris, France (d. 1937)
Giuseppe Ferrata, Italian composer and pianist, born in Gradoli, Duchy of Castro, Papal States (d. 1928)
Charles Edward Montague, English journalist and author (Fiery Particles), born in Twickenham, England (d. 1928)
Jeanne Lanvin, French fashion designer and perfumer (Lavin), born in Paris, France (d. 1946)
Lew Fields [Moses Schoenfeld], Polish-American vaudeville and film comedian (Weber and Fields), and theatrical producer, born in Poland (d. 1941)
Snitz Edwards [Edward Neumann], Hungarian-American stage and silent film actor ("Phantom of the Opera"; "College"), born in Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1937)
Albert Griffiths ("Young Griffo"), Australian boxer (World Featherweight champion 1890-92), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1927)
Mariano Azuela, Mexican novelist (The Flies, The Bosses), born in Lagos de Moreno, Jalisco (d. 1952)
Frank Knox, American politician (Republican VP candidate 1936), newspaper editor and Secretary of the Navy during World War II, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1944)
Gustave Whitehead, German-American aviation pioneer who claimed to have flown a powered airplane before the Wright brothers in 1903, born in Leutershausen, German Empire (d. 1927)[1][2][3]
Hugo Leichtentritt, German Jewish composer and musicologist, born in Pleschen, Poland (d. 1951)
Charles Rist, French economist who opposed the Keynesian Revolution, born in Lausanne, Switzerland (d. 1955)
Frank Druce, English cricket batsman (5 Tests, 1 x 50; Surrey CCC), born in London, England (d. 1954)
Harriet Brooks, Canadian physicist, born in Exeter, Ontario (d. 1933)
Johan Coenraad Altorf, Dutch sculptor (October 3rd monument), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1955)
Agner Krarup Erlang, Danish scientist and engineer known for his work on queueing theory, born in Lonborg, Denmark (d. 1929)
Bobby Walthour Sr., American cyclist (motor-pacing World Championships gold 1904-05; Madison Square Garden 6-Day Race 1901, 03), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1949)
Edwin Franko Goldman, American march music composer (founder of American Bandmasters Association), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1956)
Emile Argand, Swiss geologist (Dekbladen Theory), born in Eaux-Vives, Switzerland (d. 1940)
Ernest Jones, Welsh psychoanalyst (biographer of Freud - Life & Work of Sigmund Freud), born in Gowerton, Wales (d. 1958)
William Fox [Wilhem Fuchs], Hungarian-American movie theater pioneer and film producer (Fox Film Corporation), born in Tolcsva, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1952)
Edie Martin, British actress (Titfield Thunderbolt), born in London, England (d. 1964)
Carry van Bruggen [de Haan], Dutch author (Heleen, Eva), born in Smilde, Netherlands (d. 1932)
Vajiravudh, 6th King of Siam from the Chakri dynasty (1910-25), born in Bangkok, Siam (d. 1925)
Federigo Tozzi, Italian writer and journalist (La Torre, Tre Croci), born in Siena, Italy (d. 1920)
Ichirō Hatoyama, Japanese politician, Prime Minister (1954-56), born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 1959)
Mary Forbes [Ethel Young], British-American stage and screen character actress (Terror by Night; The Picture of Dorian Gray), born in Hornsey England (d. 1974)
Roland Diggle, English-American composer, organist and choirmaster (St. John's Episcopal Church of Los Angeles, California), born in London, England (d. 1954)
Wilhelm Canaris, German admiral and head of German military intelligence (executed for opposing Hitler), born in Aplerbeck, Germany (d. 1945)
John Garand, Canadian-American designer of the M1 semi-automatic rifle, born in St. Rémi, Quebec (d. 1974)[1]
Alexander Smallens, Russian-born American conductor, born in St Petersburg, Russia (d. 1972)
Charles Bickford, American actor (Dynamite, The Virginian), born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 1967)
Tadeusz Jarecki, Polish composer and conductor, born in Lemberg, Austria-Hungary (d. 1955)
Tom Dugan, Irish actor (Circus Clown, Drag, Skyway), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1955)
Alice Tissot, French actress (Italian Straw Hat), born in Paris, France (d. 1971)
Anton Melik, Slovenian geographer and founder of the Anton Melik Geographical Institute (The Geography of Slovenia), born in Črna Vas, Austrio-Hungarian Empire (d. 1966)[1]
Florence Lawrence, Canadian silent screen actress (Confidence), born in Hamilton, Ontario (d. 1938)
Frank Pettingell, English actor (Gaslight, Goose Steps Out), born in Liverpool (d. 1966)
Artur Rodzinski, Polish conductor (Cleveland Orchestra, 1933-43; New York Philharmonic, 1943-47; Chicago Symphony, 1947-48), born in Split, Kingdom of Dalmatia (d. 1958)
Miklós Radnai, Hungarian composer (Symphony of the Magyars), music writer, and critic, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1935)
Barney Stanley, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame forward (Stanley Cup 1915, Vancouver Millionaires; coach Chicago Black Hawks 1927-28), born in Edmonton, Alberta (d. 1971)
Satyendra Nath Bose, Indian physicist (Bose–Einstein statistics), born in Calcutta, British India (d. 1974)
Nathaniel Shilkret, American conductor and musician (Victor Salon Orchestra), born in New York City (d. 1982)
Maurice Jacobson, British concert pianist, composer, and music publisher, born in London, England (d. 1976)
Teinosuke Kinugasa, Japanese actor and director (Gate of Hell, A Page of Madness, Jujiro, Jigoku-mon), born in Mie Ken, Japan (d. 1982)
Yitzhak Edel, Israeli composer, born in Warsaw, Russian Poland (d. 1973)
Walter Greaza, American actor (Treasury Man, Martin Kane Private Eye), born in Saint Paul, Minnesota (d. 1973)
Viktor Ullmann, Austrian pianist, conductor, and composer (Der Kaiser von Atlantis), born in Teschen, Silesia (d. 1944)
Juozas Tallat-Kelpsa, Lithuanian composer and conductor, born in Kalnujai, Lithuania (d. 1949)
Lev Kuleshov, Russian filmmaker and film theorist (Po Zakonu, founder of Moscow film school), born in Tambov, Russian Empire (d. 1970)
Raymond Loucheur, French composer, born in Tourcoing, France (d. 1979)
Chiune Sugihara, Japanese diplomat (Vice-consul for the Japanese Empire in Lithuania, 1939-41), who issued thousands of travel visas to imperiled Jews, born in Kozuchi Town, Empire of Japan (d. 1986)
Hubert van Doorne, Dutch auto manufacturer (DAF Trucks), born in America, Dutch Limburg, Netherlands (d. 1979)
Xavier Cugat, Spanish American bandleader (married Abbe Lane, and Charo), born in Girona, Catalonia (d. 1990)
Century Milstead, American College Football Hall of Fame tackle (Yale; NFL C'ship 1927, NY Giants), born in Allegheny, Pennsylvania (d. 1963)
Buster Nupen, South African cricket all-rounder (17 Tests, 50 wickets; one eye only), born near Alesund, Norway (d. 1977)
Ethan Allen, American baseball center fielder (inventor Cadaco-Ellis board game All Star Baseball; Yale University), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1993)
